subject: ap-chemistry
title: HCNO/HNCO Isomers: Bonding, Thermodynamics & Kinetics
stimulus:
Answer the following questions about the isomers fulminic acid and isocyanic acid.
Two possible Lewis electron-dot diagrams for fulminic acid, HCNO, are shown below.

parts.prompt:
Explain why the diagram on the left is the better representation for the bonding in fulminic acid. Justify your
choice based on formal charges.
parts.scoring_notes: The better diagram minimizes formal charges (or places negative formal charges on the more electronegative atom). In the left diagram, formal charges are closer to zero on all atoms, making it the preferred Lewis structure per formal charge rules.

scoring_points.description: For correct formal charge assignment showing the left diagram has smaller/zero formal charges: 1 point

required_elements: Calculate formal charges for both diagrams.
The left diagram has formal charges closer to zero (or negative charge on the more electronegative atom).
alternatives.correct_example: In the left diagram, C has a formal charge of 0, N has −1, O has +1 (or similar distribution depending on which structure is 'left'). In the right diagram, C has a larger formal charge. The best Lewis structure has the smallest formal charges and places negative formal charges on the most electronegative atoms. The left diagram satisfies this criterion better.

wrong_examples: Picking the wrong diagram — must calculate formal charges (FC = valence electrons − nonbonding electrons − ½ bonding electrons) for each atom in each structure.
common_traps: Formal charge = (valence e$^-$) − (lone pair e$^-$) − (½ × bonding e$^-$). Best structure: all FCs as close to zero as possible; negative FC on most electronegative atom.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for a correct assignment of formal charges in the two diagrams AND 1 point is earned for a correct explanation. In the left diagram (H–C≡N–O:), C has formal charge 0 and O has formal charge −1; in the right diagram (H–C=N=O), C has formal charge +1 and O has formal charge 0. The left diagram is the better representation because it places the negative formal charge on oxygen, which is more electronegative than carbon.

sample_responses.response: In the left diagram the C atom has a formal charge of 0 and the O atom has a formal charge of −1. In the right diagram the C atom has a formal charge of +1 and the O atom has a formal charge of 0. The left diagram is the better representation because it places the negative formal charge on oxygen, which is more electronegative than carbon.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly assigns formal charges on both Lewis structures and justifies the preferred structure using the electronegativity-of-the-more-electronegative-atom rule — the CB model answer.

sample_responses.response: The left diagram is better because it has a triple bond, which is stronger than double bonds.
sample_responses.commentary: Doesn't address formal charges as the question requires; bond strength alone isn't the criterion for choosing the preferred Lewis structure here.

parts.prompt:
Using the Lewis electron-dot diagrams of fulminic acid and isocyanic acid shown in the boxes above and the
table of average bond enthalpies below, determine the value of ΔH° for the reaction of HCNO(g) to form
HNCO(g).
Bond enthalpies: N−O: 201 kJ/mol; C=N: 615 kJ/mol; C≡N: 891 kJ/mol; H−C: 413 kJ/mol; H−N: 391 kJ/mol; C=O: 745 kJ/mol
parts.scoring_notes: ΔH° = Σ(bonds broken) − Σ(bonds formed). HCNO (fulminic): H–C + C≡N + N=O → 413 + 891 + 201 = 1505 kJ broken. HNCO (isocyanic): H–N + N=C + C=O → 391 + 615 + 745 = 1751 kJ formed. ΔH° = 1505 − 1751 = −246 kJ/mol.

scoring_points.description: For correct ΔH° using bond enthalpy method: 1 point

required_elements: Correct bond identification from Lewis structures.
alternatives.correct_example:
Bonds broken (HCNO): H–C (413) + C≡N (891) + N=O (201) = 1505 kJ/mol
Bonds formed (HNCO): H–N (391) + N=C (615) + C=O (745) = 1751 kJ/mol
ΔH° = 1505 − 1751 = −246 kJ/mol

wrong_examples: Subtracting in the wrong order — bonds formed are released (negative); ΔH° = bonds broken − bonds formed.
Using wrong bonds from structures — must read bonds from the Lewis diagrams.
common_traps: Bond enthalpy: ΔH° = ΣD(bonds broken) − ΣD(bonds formed). Bonds broken in reactants; bonds formed in products.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for subtracting the enthalpies of bonds formed from the enthalpies of bonds broken AND 1 point is earned for the correct determination of $\Delta H°$.

sample_responses.response: Bonds broken (HCNO): H–C + C≡N + N–O = 413 + 891 + 201 = 1505 kJ/mol. Bonds formed (HNCO): H–N + N=C + C=O = 391 + 615 + 745 = 1751 kJ/mol. $\Delta H° = 1505 - 1751 = -246\text{ kJ/mol}$.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly reads the bonds from each Lewis structure, sums bond enthalpies separately for reactants and products, and subtracts (broken − formed) to get the CB model answer of −246 kJ/mol.

Subtracts in the wrong order — the convention is $\Delta H° = \Sigma D(\text{broken}) - \Sigma D(\text{formed})$, which gives a negative value here since more energy is released when the stronger bonds in HNCO form.

parts.prompt: A student claims that ΔS° for the reaction is close to zero. Explain why the student's claim is accurate.
parts.scoring_notes: HCNO(g) → HNCO(g) is an isomerization: same molecular formula, same number of gas-phase molecules (1 mol gas → 1 mol gas), same phase. No change in number of moles of gas → ΔS° ≈ 0.

scoring_points.description: For explaining that the reaction involves the same number of gas molecules with the same formula: 1 point

required_elements: Both reactant and product are gases with the same molecular formula.
No change in number of moles of gas → no significant change in disorder → ΔS° ≈ 0.
alternatives.correct_example: HCNO(g) and HNCO(g) have the same molecular formula (isomers) and both are gases. The reaction converts 1 mole of gas to 1 mole of gas with no change in phase, no change in number of particles, and no significant change in molecular complexity. Therefore ΔS° is very close to zero — there is essentially no change in the disorder of the system.

wrong_examples: Saying ΔS° is large — an isomerization with 1 gaseous molecule on each side has nearly zero ΔS°.
common_traps: ΔS° is primarily affected by: change in moles of gas, phase changes, and dissolution. Here: no change in any of these → ΔS° ≈ 0.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for a correct explanation: the change from fulminic acid to isocyanic acid is a rearrangement of atoms with no change in phase or number of molecules.

sample_responses.response: The reaction is a rearrangement of the same atoms with no change in phase and no change in the number of gas molecules (1 mol HCNO(g) → 1 mol HNCO(g)), so $\Delta S°$ is approximately zero.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies that an isomerization with the same phase and same mole count has essentially no entropy change — the CB model answer.

sample_responses.response: $\Delta S°$ is near zero because entropy is always low for gases.
sample_responses.commentary: Entropy of gases is generally high, not low — the correct reasoning is that the reaction produces the same number of gas-phase molecules with the same formula, so there is no significant change in disorder.

parts.prompt:
Which species, fulminic acid (HCNO) or isocyanic acid (HNCO), is present in higher concentration at
equilibrium at 298 K? Justify your answer in terms of thermodynamic favorability and the equilibrium
constant.
parts.scoring_notes: ΔG° ≈ ΔH° − TΔS° ≈ −246 − (298)(0) = −246 kJ/mol (since ΔS° ≈ 0). ΔG° < 0 → forward reaction is spontaneous → K > 1 → [HNCO] > [HCNO] at equilibrium. Isocyanic acid (HNCO) is in higher concentration.

scoring_points.description: For HNCO with ΔG° < 0 → K > 1 reasoning: 1 point

required_elements: ΔG° ≈ ΔH° ≈ −246 kJ/mol (negative) → forward reaction spontaneous → K > 1 → products predominate.
alternatives.correct_example: Isocyanic acid (HNCO) is present in higher concentration. Since ΔS° ≈ 0, $\Delta G° \approx \Delta H° = -246\text{ kJ/mol}$. A negative ΔG° means the forward reaction is thermodynamically favorable (K > 1 from $\Delta G° = -RT\ln K$), so at equilibrium the product HNCO predominates over the reactant HCNO.

wrong_examples: HCNO — negative ΔG° means products are favored at equilibrium.
common_traps: $\Delta G° < 0 \Rightarrow K > 1 \Rightarrow$ products predominate at equilibrium.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the correct choice with a valid justification (calculation of $\Delta G°$ is a sufficient justification) AND 1 point is earned for correctly connecting thermodynamic favorability to the equilibrium constant $K$. Because $\Delta S° \approx 0$, $\Delta G° \approx \Delta H° \approx -246\text{ kJ/mol}$; from $\Delta G° = -RT\ln K$, a negative $\Delta G°$ yields $K > 1$, so products predominate.

sample_responses.response: Isocyanic acid (HNCO) will be present in higher concentration. $\Delta G° \approx \Delta H°$ because $\Delta S°$ is essentially zero, so $\Delta G° \approx -246\text{ kJ/mol}$ — the forward reaction is thermodynamically favorable. Since $\Delta G° < 0$, $K > 1$ (from $\Delta G° = -RT\ln K$), so [HNCO] > [HCNO] at equilibrium.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies HNCO, supports with a calculated $\Delta G°$, and connects the sign of $\Delta G°$ to the magnitude of $K$ — the CB model answer.

sample_responses.response: HCNO will be in higher concentration because it is the reactant.
sample_responses.commentary: Treats the reactant as the majority species without any thermodynamic analysis; $\Delta H°$ is strongly negative and $\Delta S° \approx 0$, giving a negative $\Delta G°$ and $K > 1$, so the product HNCO predominates.

parts.prompt:
The student proposes that the rate law is rate = k[CO(NH$_2$)$_2$].
(i) Explain how the data support the student's proposed rate law.
(ii) Using the proposed rate law and the student's results, determine the value of the rate constant, k.
Include units with your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (e)(i): The data (concentration vs. time table/graph) show a linear ln[urea] vs. t plot (consistent with first-order), OR that the half-life is constant. First-order rate law: rate = k[urea]. Part (e)(ii): For first order, k = 0.693/t$_{1/2}$, or k = −slope of ln[urea] vs. t. Units: s$^{-1}$ or min$^{-1}$.

scoring_points.description: For explaining data consistency with first-order kinetics: 1 point

required_elements: The ln[urea] vs. time graph is linear (or half-life is constant), consistent with a first-order rate law.
alternatives.correct_example: For a first-order reaction, a plot of ln[urea] vs. time is linear. The student's data show a linear relationship on the ln[urea] vs. t plot, consistent with rate = k[CO(NH$_2$)$_2$] (first order in urea).

wrong_examples: Citing [urea] vs. t as linear — that would indicate zero order.
common_traps: First-order diagnostic: constant half-life OR linear ln[A] vs. t plot.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for a correct explanation. From inspection of the data table or graph it is evident that the decomposition reaction has a constant half-life, which indicates that the reaction is first-order.

sample_responses.response: From the data, [urea] drops from 0.100 M to 0.050 M in 10 hours, and from 0.050 M to 0.025 M in another 10 hours — the half-life is constant at about 10 hours. A constant half-life is the diagnostic for first-order kinetics, consistent with the proposed rate law.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies two successive half-lives from the data table and correctly links constant half-life to first-order kinetics — the CB model answer.

sample_responses.response: The data show [urea] decreases linearly with time, which is typical of first-order reactions.
sample_responses.commentary: A linear [A]-vs.-t plot indicates zero-order kinetics, not first-order; first-order gives a linear ln[A]-vs.-t plot or a constant half-life.

scoring_points.description: For the correct k with units: 1 point

required_elements: Correct numerical value consistent with the data; units = min$^{-1}$ or s$^{-1}$.
alternatives.correct_example: From the data: $k = -\text{slope of ln[urea] vs. t}$. Read the slope from the graph and report with units of (time)$^{-1}$.

wrong_examples: Units of M$^{-1}$s$^{-1}$ — those are second-order units; first order uses s$^{-1}$.
common_traps: First-order rate constant k has units of time$^{-1}$.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the correct value of $k$ with correct units: $k = \frac{0.693}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{10\text{ h}} = 0.069\text{ h}^{-1}$, OR from integrated first-order form $k = \frac{\ln[A]_0 - \ln[A]_t}{t} = \frac{\ln(0.1000) - \ln(0.0500)}{10\text{ h}} = 0.069\text{ h}^{-1}$.

sample_responses.commentary: Uses the first-order half-life relationship with the constant $t_{1/2} \approx 10$ h from the data and reports $k$ with correct reciprocal-time units — the CB model answer.
Correct magnitude but wrong units; first-order rate constants have units of time$^{-1}$ (h$^{-1}$), not M$^{-1}$time$^{-1}$ (which would be second-order).

parts.prompt:
The student learns that the decomposition reaction was run in a solution with a pH of 13. Briefly describe
an experiment, including the initial conditions that you would change and the data you would gather, to
determine whether the rate of the reaction depends on the concentration of OH$^-$(aq).
parts.scoring_notes: Run the experiment at different [OH$^-$] values (different pH), keeping [urea] constant. Measure reaction rates at each [OH$^-$]. If rate changes proportionally to [OH$^-$], the reaction depends on [OH$^-$]. If rate is unchanged, it does not.

scoring_points.description: For specifying varying [OH$^-$] while keeping [urea] constant: 1 point

required_elements: Run multiple experiments at different pH values (different [OH$^-$]) while holding [urea] constant.
alternatives.correct_example: Prepare several solutions with the same initial [urea] but different concentrations of OH$^-$ (e.g., adjust pH to 12, 13, 14 by adding NaOH). Keep all other conditions constant.

wrong_examples: Varying both [urea] and [OH$^-$] simultaneously — cannot isolate the effect of [OH$^-$] if [urea] also changes.
common_traps: To isolate the effect of one variable, hold all others constant (method of initial rates).
scoring_points.official_rubric: The CB scoring guideline awards a single point for the description of a valid experiment — performing the experiment at a different concentration of OH$^-$ while holding other variables (such as temperature) constant. This granular point reflects the 'initial conditions changed' portion.

sample_responses.response: Prepare at least two solutions containing the same initial urea concentration but different hydroxide concentrations (for example, one at pH 12 and one at pH 14), keeping temperature constant.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the key variable to change ([OH$^-$]) and the key variables to hold constant — consistent with the CB model answer.

sample_responses.response: Run the same experiment again with different temperatures.
sample_responses.commentary: Temperature changes affect $k$ (the Arrhenius relation) but do not test the dependence on [OH$^-$]; the correct variable to change is [OH$^-$].

scoring_points.description: For specifying the data to gather (rate vs. [OH$^-$]): 1 point

required_elements: Measure the initial rate (or k) for each [OH$^-$] value.
Compare rates to determine if rate ∝ [OH$^-$] (first order in OH$^-$) or independent of [OH$^-$] (zero order in OH$^-$).
alternatives.correct_example: Measure the initial rate of urea decomposition (from [urea] vs. time data) for each [OH$^-$] condition. Compare the rates: if doubling [OH$^-$] doubles the rate, the reaction is first order in [OH$^-$]; if no change, the rate is independent of [OH$^-$].

wrong_examples: Not specifying what data to compare — must compare rates (or rate constants) across the different [OH$^-$] conditions.
common_traps: Method of initial rates: vary one concentration at a time and compare initial rates to determine the order with respect to that variable.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The CB scoring guideline combines this step with the experimental description into a single 'valid experiment' point. The required data to collect are [CO(NH$_2$)$_2$] as a function of time at each different [OH$^-$] so that the rate (or $k$) can be calculated and compared.

sample_responses.response: For each [OH$^-$] solution, measure the concentration of CO(NH$_2$)$_2$ as a function of time. Calculate the rate constant (or initial rate) at each [OH$^-$] and compare: if $k$ (or initial rate) changes with [OH$^-$], the rate depends on [OH$^-$]; if not, it does not.
sample_responses.commentary: Specifies the correct data to gather (concentration vs. time at each [OH$^-$]) and how to analyze (compare rates or rate constants) — consistent with the CB model answer.

sample_responses.response: Measure pH at the end of the reaction.
sample_responses.commentary: Final pH alone does not reveal how rate depends on [OH$^-$]; the key is to track concentration-vs.-time (rate) at different initial [OH$^-$] values.
